cards.front: What is power in physics?
cards.back: The rate at which energy is transferred or work is done.
cards.explanation: The specification defines power in either way: the rate of energy transfer or the rate of doing work.

choices.text: The total energy stored in an object.
choices.feedback: Power is defined as a rate, not as a total amount of stored energy.

choices.text: The rate at which energy is transferred or work is done.
choices.feedback: Correct: both energy transfer and work done can define power as a rate.

choices.text: The force applied to move an object.
choices.feedback: The definition uses the rate of energy transfer or work done, not force alone.

choices.text: The time taken to transfer any energy.
choices.feedback: Power is a rate of transfer or work, not the elapsed time itself.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 121-127; 6.1.1.4 Power
sources.excerpt:
Power is defined as the rate at which energy is transferred or the
rate at which work is done.

cards.front: During changes in a system, what may happen to energy, and what is forbidden by conservation of energy?
cards.back: Energy can be transferred usefully, stored or dissipated, but it cannot be created or destroyed.
cards.explanation: Transfer, storage and dissipation are allowed descriptions of energy; creation and destruction are not.

choices.text: Energy can be transferred or stored, but dissipation destroys it.
choices.feedback: Dissipation is listed as something that can happen to energy; the energy is not destroyed.

choices.text: Energy may be created when useful and destroyed when wasted.
choices.feedback: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, whether transferred usefully or dissipated.

choices.text: Energy can be transferred usefully, stored or dissipated, but it cannot be created or destroyed.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the permitted changes and the conservation limit.

choices.text: Energy can only remain in its original store.
choices.feedback: Energy may be transferred, stored in another way or dissipated.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 121-127; 6.1.2.1 Energy transfers in a system
sources.excerpt:
Energy can be transferred usefully, stored or dissipated, but cannot
be created or destroyed.

cards.front: In this specification, what makes an energy resource renewable?
cards.back: It is being, or can be, replenished as it is used.
cards.explanation: The definition includes a resource that is either being replenished or can be replenished as it is used.

choices.text: It is guaranteed never to run out, whatever the rate of use.
choices.feedback: The definition is replenishment as the resource is used, not an unlimited-use guarantee.

choices.text: It is being, or can be, replenished as it is used.
choices.feedback: Correct: this preserves both parts of the specification's definition.

choices.text: It converts all input energy into useful output.
choices.feedback: That concerns energy conversion, not whether the resource is replenished as it is used.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 121-127; 6.1.3 National and global energy resources
sources.excerpt:
A renewable energy resource is one that is being (or can be)
replenished as it is used.

cards.front: How does a material's thermal conductivity affect the rate of energy transfer by conduction across it?
cards.back: The higher the thermal conductivity, the higher the rate of energy transfer by conduction.
cards.explanation: Thermal conductivity and the rate of energy transfer by conduction increase together.

choices.text: The higher the thermal conductivity, the lower the rate of energy transfer by conduction.
choices.feedback: This reverses the stated relationship: a higher conductivity gives a higher transfer rate.

choices.text: Thermal conductivity has no effect on the rate of energy transfer by conduction.
choices.feedback: The specification directly links higher conductivity to a higher conduction rate.

choices.text: The higher the thermal conductivity, the higher the rate of energy transfer by conduction.
choices.feedback: Correct: both quantities increase together.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 121-127; 6.1.2.1 Energy transfers in a system
sources.excerpt:
The higher the thermal conductivity of a material the higher the rate
of energy transfer by conduction across the material.

cards.front: What is electric current, and what determines its size?
cards.back: Electric current is a flow of electrical charge; its size is the rate of flow of charge.
cards.explanation: Current describes flowing electrical charge, while its size describes how rapidly that charge flows.

choices.text: Electric current is a flow of electrical charge; its size is the rate of flow of charge.
choices.feedback: Correct: current is charge flow and its size is a rate.

choices.text: Electric current is electrical charge stored in a component; its size is the stored charge.
choices.feedback: Current is defined as a flow of charge, not charge held in storage.

choices.text: Electric current is a flow of charge; its size is the total charge, regardless of time.
choices.feedback: The size of current is the rate of charge flow, not merely the total charge.

choices.text: Electric current is the source of potential difference in a circuit.
choices.feedback: The stated definition is a flow of electrical charge, not a source of potential difference.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 127-134; 6.2.1.2 Electrical charge and current
sources.excerpt:
Electric current is a flow of electrical charge. The size of the electric
current is the rate of flow of electrical charge.

cards.front: For a fixed potential difference across a component, what happens to current as resistance increases?
cards.back: The current decreases.
cards.explanation: For a given potential difference, greater component resistance means a smaller current.

choices.text: The current increases.
choices.feedback: This reverses the relationship: greater resistance gives a smaller current for a given potential difference.

choices.text: The current decreases.
choices.feedback: Correct: increasing resistance reduces current when potential difference is fixed.

choices.text: The current stays the same.
choices.feedback: The specification states that current becomes smaller as resistance becomes greater.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 127-134; 6.2.1.3 Current, resistance and potential difference
sources.excerpt:
The greater the resistance of the component
the smaller the current for a given potential difference (pd) across
the component.

cards.front: How does current compare through components connected in series?
cards.back: The same current flows through each component.
cards.explanation: A series connection has one current value through every component.

choices.text: The supply current is divided equally between the components.
choices.feedback: The current is not divided in series; it is the same through each component.

choices.text: The same current flows through each component.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the series-current rule.

choices.text: The current becomes smaller after each component.
choices.feedback: Position in the series circuit does not change the stated current value.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 127-134; 6.2.2 Series and parallel circuits
sources.excerpt:
For components connected in series:
• there is the same current through each component

cards.front: What are the frequency and approximate potential difference of the UK domestic electricity supply?
cards.back: 50 Hz and about 230 V.
cards.explanation: The frequency is given exactly as 50 Hz, while the potential difference is qualified as about 230 V.

choices.text: 60 Hz and about 230 V.
choices.feedback: The potential difference matches, but the stated frequency is 50 Hz.

choices.text: 50 Hz and about 120 V.
choices.feedback: The frequency matches, but the stated potential difference is about 230 V.

choices.text: 50 Hz and about 230 V.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the specification values for the UK domestic supply.

choices.text: 60 Hz and about 120 V.
choices.feedback: Both values differ from the stated 50 Hz and about 230 V.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 127-134; 6.2.3.1 Direct and alternating potential difference
sources.excerpt:
In the United Kingdom the
domestic electricity supply has a frequency of 50 Hz and is about
230 V.

cards.front: In the required density practical, how should volume be found for a regularly shaped solid and for an irregularly shaped solid?
cards.back: Use dimensions for a regular object and a displacement technique for an irregular object.
cards.explanation: The volume method depends on shape: dimensions are used for regular objects and displacement for irregular objects.

choices.text: Use displacement for a regular object and dimensions for an irregular object.
choices.feedback: The methods are reversed: dimensions are for regular objects and displacement is for irregular objects.

choices.text: Use dimensions for a regular object and a displacement technique for an irregular object.
choices.feedback: Correct: these are the two specified volume methods.

choices.text: Use dimensions for both regular and irregular objects.
choices.feedback: The irregular object's volume should be found by a displacement technique.

choices.text: Use a displacement technique for both regular and irregular objects.
choices.feedback: The regular object's volume should be determined from its dimensions.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 135-138; 6.3.1.1 Density of materials
sources.excerpt:
Volume should
be determined from the dimensions of regularly shaped objects, and by a displacement technique
for irregularly shaped objects.

cards.front: What happens to a substance's mass when it melts, freezes, boils, evaporates, condenses or sublimates?
cards.back: Its mass is conserved.
cards.explanation: Mass remains conserved during each of the listed changes of state.

choices.text: Its mass always increases.
choices.feedback: The specification states that mass is conserved during these state changes.

choices.text: Its mass always decreases.
choices.feedback: The specification states that mass is conserved during these state changes.

choices.text: Its mass is conserved.
choices.feedback: Correct: the listed changes of state conserve mass.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 135-138; 6.3.1.2 Changes of state
sources.excerpt:
Students should be able to describe how, when substances change
state (melt, freeze, boil, evaporate, condense or sublimate), mass is
conserved.

cards.front: What is the internal energy of a system?
cards.back: The total kinetic energy and potential energy of all the particles that make up the system.
cards.explanation: Internal energy totals both the kinetic and potential energy of every atom or molecule in the system.

choices.text: Only the kinetic energy of the system's particles.
choices.feedback: Internal energy includes potential energy as well as kinetic energy.

choices.text: Only the potential energy of the system's particles.
choices.feedback: Internal energy includes kinetic energy as well as potential energy.

choices.text: Only energy transferred to the particles while heating.
choices.feedback: The definition is the total kinetic and potential energy of all the particles.

choices.text: The total kinetic energy and potential energy of all the particles that make up the system.
choices.feedback: Correct: both forms of particle energy are included in the total.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 135-138; 6.3.2.1 Internal energy
sources.excerpt:
Internal energy is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of all
the particles (atoms and molecules) that make up a system.

cards.front: When energy is supplied during a change of state, what changes and what stays unchanged?
cards.back: The internal energy changes, but the temperature does not.
cards.explanation: During the state change, supplied energy changes the system's internal energy without changing its temperature.

choices.text: The internal energy changes, but the temperature does not.
choices.feedback: Correct: the supplied energy changes internal energy rather than temperature.

choices.text: The temperature changes, but the internal energy does not.
choices.feedback: This reverses the stated result: internal energy changes while temperature does not.

choices.text: Both the internal energy and temperature increase.
choices.feedback: The temperature does not change during the stated change of state.

choices.text: Neither the internal energy nor the temperature changes.
choices.feedback: The temperature stays unchanged, but the supplied energy changes internal energy.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 135-138; 6.3.2.3 Changes of state and specific latent heat
sources.excerpt:
When a change of state occurs, the energy supplied changes
the energy stored (internal energy) but not the temperature.

cards.front: What is the basic structure of an atom?
cards.back: A positively charged nucleus of protons and neutrons, surrounded by negatively charged electrons.
cards.explanation: Protons and neutrons compose the positively charged nucleus, while negatively charged electrons surround it.

choices.text: A negatively charged nucleus of electrons and neutrons, surrounded by positive protons.
choices.feedback: The nucleus is positively charged and contains protons and neutrons; negative electrons surround it.

choices.text: A positively charged nucleus of protons and neutrons, surrounded by negatively charged electrons.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives the stated particles, charges and arrangement.

choices.text: A positively charged nucleus containing protons only, surrounded by neutrons and electrons.
choices.feedback: The nucleus is composed of both protons and neutrons, and the surrounding particles are electrons.

choices.text: Positive charge and negative electrons spread uniformly throughout the atom.
choices.feedback: The stated basic structure concentrates protons and neutrons in a nucleus with electrons around it.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 138-143; 6.4.1.1 The structure of an atom
sources.excerpt:
The basic structure of an atom is a positively charged nucleus
composed of both protons and neutrons surrounded by negatively
charged electrons.

cards.front: What are isotopes of an element?
cards.back: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
cards.explanation: Isotopes retain the identity of the same element but differ in their numbers of neutrons.

choices.text: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of protons.
choices.feedback: The stated difference between isotopes is neutron number, not proton number.

choices.text: Atoms of different elements with the same number of neutrons.
choices.feedback: Isotopes are atoms of the same element, not different elements.

choices.text: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
choices.feedback: Correct: same element, different neutron numbers.

choices.text: Positive ions formed when atoms lose outer electrons.
choices.feedback: Losing electrons describes ion formation, whereas isotopes differ in neutron number.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 138-143; 6.4.1.2 Mass number, atomic number and isotopes
sources.excerpt:
Atoms of the same element can have different numbers of
neutrons; these atoms are called isotopes of that element.

cards.front: What is radioactive decay?
cards.back: A random process in which an unstable nucleus gives out radiation as it changes to become more stable.
cards.explanation: An unstable nucleus emits radiation as it becomes more stable, and this process is random.

choices.text: A random process in which an unstable nucleus gives out radiation as it changes to become more stable.
choices.feedback: Correct: this includes instability, radiation emission, increased stability and randomness.

choices.text: A predictable process in which a stable nucleus absorbs radiation.
choices.feedback: Radioactive decay is random and involves an unstable nucleus giving out radiation.

choices.text: The loss of outer electrons by an atom.
choices.feedback: The process described concerns radiation from an unstable nucleus, not outer electrons.

choices.text: A change in which an unstable nucleus becomes less stable without emitting radiation.
choices.feedback: The nucleus gives out radiation as it changes to become more stable.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 138-143; 6.4.2.1 Radioactive decay and nuclear radiation
sources.excerpt:
Some atomic nuclei are unstable. The nucleus gives out radiation
as it changes to become more stable. This is a random process
called radioactive decay.

cards.front: When does a force do work on an object?
cards.back: When the force causes a displacement of the object.
cards.explanation: The force must cause the object's displacement for work to be done on it.

choices.text: Whenever any force acts, even if there is no displacement.
choices.feedback: The stated condition requires the force to cause a displacement.

choices.text: When the force causes a displacement of the object.
choices.feedback: Correct: a force does work when it causes displacement.

choices.text: Whenever the object moves, whether or not the force causes the displacement.
choices.feedback: The displacement must be caused by the force in question.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 143-155; 6.5.2 Work done and energy transfer
sources.excerpt:
When a force causes an object to move through a distance work is
done on the object. So a force does work on an object when the
force causes a displacement of the object.

cards.front: According to Newton's Second Law, how does acceleration depend on resultant force and mass?
cards.back: It is proportional to resultant force and inversely proportional to mass.
cards.explanation: Acceleration has a direct proportional relationship with resultant force and an inverse proportional relationship with mass.

choices.text: It is inversely proportional to resultant force and proportional to mass.
choices.feedback: This reverses both relationships given by Newton's Second Law.

choices.text: It is proportional to both resultant force and mass.
choices.feedback: Acceleration is inversely, not directly, proportional to mass.

choices.text: It is proportional to resultant force and inversely proportional to mass.
choices.feedback: Correct: this states both proportional relationships.

choices.text: It is independent of both resultant force and mass.
choices.feedback: Newton's Second Law explicitly relates acceleration to both quantities.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 143-155; 6.5.4.2.2 Newton's Second Law
sources.excerpt:
The acceleration of an object is proportional to the resultant force
acting on the object, and inversely proportional to the mass of the
object.

cards.front: Which wave type has compressions and rarefactions, and what example is given?
cards.back: Longitudinal waves; sound waves travelling through air are an example.
cards.explanation: Longitudinal waves have compressions and rarefactions, with sound in air as the example; water-surface ripples are transverse.

choices.text: Longitudinal waves; sound waves travelling through air are an example.
choices.feedback: Correct: both the feature and example match longitudinal waves.

choices.text: Transverse waves; sound waves travelling through air are an example.
choices.feedback: Sound waves travelling through air are stated to be longitudinal.

choices.text: Longitudinal waves; ripples on a water surface are an example.
choices.feedback: Water-surface ripples are given as a transverse-wave example.

choices.text: Transverse waves; both sound in air and water ripples are examples.
choices.feedback: Only the water-surface ripples are identified as transverse; sound in air is longitudinal.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 155-158; 6.6.1.1 Transverse and longitudinal waves
sources.excerpt:
The ripples on a water surface are an example of a transverse
wave.
Longitudinal waves show areas of compression and rarefaction.
Sound waves travelling through air are longitudinal.

cards.front: What is the amplitude of a wave?
cards.back: The maximum displacement of a point on the wave from its undisturbed position.
cards.explanation: Amplitude measures the greatest displacement from the wave's undisturbed position.

choices.text: The distance between equivalent points on adjacent waves.
choices.feedback: Amplitude is measured from the undisturbed position, not between adjacent waves.

choices.text: The maximum displacement of a point on the wave from its undisturbed position.
choices.feedback: Correct: maximum displacement from the undisturbed position defines amplitude.

choices.text: The number of waves passing a point each second.
choices.feedback: Amplitude is a displacement, not a count of waves passing a point.

choices.text: The speed at which energy moves through the medium.
choices.feedback: Amplitude is maximum displacement, not wave speed.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 155-158; 6.6.1.2 Properties of waves
sources.excerpt:
The amplitude of a wave is the maximum displacement of a point
on a wave away from its undisturbed position.

cards.front: What is the frequency of a wave?
cards.back: The number of waves passing a point each second.
cards.explanation: Frequency counts how many waves pass a chosen point in each second.

choices.text: The maximum displacement from the undisturbed position.
choices.feedback: Frequency is a number of waves per second, not a displacement.

choices.text: The distance between equivalent points on adjacent waves.
choices.feedback: Frequency counts passing waves rather than measuring a distance.

choices.text: The number of waves passing a point each second.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the definition of frequency.

choices.text: The speed at which wave energy moves through a medium.
choices.feedback: Frequency is a count per second, not the wave's speed.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 155-158; 6.6.1.2 Properties of waves
sources.excerpt:
The frequency of a wave is the number of waves passing a point
each second.

cards.front: What is the order of electromagnetic-wave groups from long to short wavelength?
cards.back: Radio, microwave, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays.
cards.explanation: This long-to-short-wavelength sequence also runs from low to high frequency.

choices.text: Gamma rays, X-rays, ultraviolet, visible light, infrared, microwave, radio.
choices.feedback: This is the reverse of the requested long-to-short-wavelength order.

choices.text: Radio, infrared, microwave, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays.
choices.feedback: Microwave comes before infrared in the stated sequence.

choices.text: Radio, microwave, infrared, ultraviolet, visible light, X-rays, gamma rays.
choices.feedback: Visible light comes before ultraviolet in the stated sequence.

choices.text: Radio, microwave, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma rays.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the complete long-to-short-wavelength order.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 155-158; 6.6.2.1 Types of electromagnetic waves
sources.excerpt:
Going from long to
short wavelength (or from low to high frequency) the groups are:
radio, microwave, infrared, visible light (red to violet), ultraviolet, X-
rays and gamma rays.

cards.front: How do like and unlike magnetic poles interact?
cards.back: Like poles repel; unlike poles attract.
cards.explanation: The interaction reverses with the pole pairing: like poles repel and unlike poles attract.

choices.text: Like poles attract; unlike poles repel.
choices.feedback: This reverses both stated interactions.

choices.text: Like poles repel; unlike poles attract.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives both pole interactions.

choices.text: Both like and unlike poles attract.
choices.feedback: Like poles repel rather than attract.

choices.text: Both like and unlike poles repel.
choices.feedback: Unlike poles attract rather than repel.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 159-162; 6.7.1.1 Poles of a magnet
sources.excerpt:
Two like poles repel each other. Two
unlike poles attract each other.

cards.front: How does a permanent magnet differ from an induced magnet?
cards.back: A permanent magnet produces its own field; an induced magnet becomes magnetic when placed in a magnetic field.
cards.explanation: The permanent magnet has its own magnetic field, while the induced material becomes a magnet through placement in a magnetic field.

choices.text: A permanent magnet becomes magnetic only in an external field; an induced magnet produces its own field.
choices.feedback: This reverses the definitions of permanent and induced magnets.

choices.text: Both magnets produce their own field without being placed in another magnetic field.
choices.feedback: An induced magnet becomes magnetic when it is placed in a magnetic field.

choices.text: A permanent magnet produces its own field; an induced magnet becomes magnetic when placed in a magnetic field.
choices.feedback: Correct: this is the stated distinction.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 159-162; 6.7.1.1 Poles of a magnet
sources.excerpt:
A permanent magnet produces its own magnetic field. An induced
magnet is a material that becomes a magnet when it is placed in a
magnetic field.

cards.front: What is a magnetic field?
cards.back: The region around a magnet where a force acts on another magnet or on a magnetic material.
cards.explanation: A magnetic field is defined as the surrounding region in which another magnet or a magnetic material experiences a force.

choices.text: The region around a magnet where a force acts on another magnet or on a magnetic material.
choices.feedback: Correct: the field is defined by the region in which this force acts.

choices.text: Only the material inside a magnet where its force is strongest.
choices.feedback: The magnetic field is the region around the magnet, not only material inside it.

choices.text: A single line joining the magnet's two poles and nowhere else.
choices.feedback: The definition describes a region around the magnet, not one isolated line.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 159-162; 6.7.1.2 Magnetic fields
sources.excerpt:
The region around a magnet where a force acts on another magnet
or on a magnetic material (iron, steel, cobalt and nickel) is called
the magnetic field.

cards.front: What is an electromagnet, and what does its iron core do?
cards.back: It is a solenoid with an iron core; the core increases the solenoid's magnetic-field strength.
cards.explanation: An electromagnet is specifically a solenoid containing an iron core, and adding that core strengthens the solenoid's magnetic field.

choices.text: It is a solenoid without a core; adding iron weakens its magnetic field.
choices.feedback: An electromagnet has an iron core, and that core increases field strength.

choices.text: It is a solenoid with an iron core; the core increases the solenoid's magnetic-field strength.
choices.feedback: Correct: this gives both the structure and the core's effect.

choices.text: It is a straight iron wire; forming a solenoid has no role.
choices.feedback: The stated definition requires a solenoid with an iron core.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 159-162; 6.7.2.1 Electromagnetism
sources.excerpt:
Adding an iron core increases the strength of the
magnetic field of a solenoid. An electromagnet is a solenoid with an
iron core.

cards.front: How do scalar and vector quantities differ?
cards.back: A scalar has magnitude only; a vector has magnitude and an associated direction.
cards.explanation: Magnitude is sufficient for a scalar, whereas a vector also has an associated direction.

choices.text: A scalar has magnitude only; a vector has magnitude and an associated direction.
choices.feedback: Correct: a vector has an associated direction as well as magnitude, while a scalar has magnitude only.

choices.text: Both scalars and vectors have magnitude only.
choices.feedback: A vector also has an associated direction.

choices.text: Both scalars and vectors have magnitude and an associated direction.
choices.feedback: A scalar has magnitude only; an associated direction is a property of a vector.

cards.subject: Physics

sources.title: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Trilogy (8464)
sources.locator: Official PDF pages 143-155; 6.5.1.1 Scalar and vector quantities
sources.excerpt:
Scalar quantities have magnitude only.
Vector quantities have magnitude and an associated direction.
